title: The Cave
description: A dream. Adachi and Shimamura walk through a cave together, hand in hand. A quiet, surreal moment of connection that lingers even after waking.
content:
I was walking through a cave with Adachi.

Once my eyes adjusted to the dark, I finally made out the uneven curves of the stone walls and realized where we were. And the moment I wondered why we were here, I understood: this was a dream.

Adachi and I were both wearing school uniforms—but not our school's. We were dressed in sailor uniforms I had never seen before. It was probably the first time I'd 'seen,' or rather imagined, Adachi in a sailor uniform… but it suited her hair color surprisingly well.

We walked hand in hand through the cave.

There was no light between our fingers—only warmth, only the feeling of her skin against mine.

Adachi was the one leading me. At first I thought, that's unusual, but when I really considered it, she was the more decisive one between us, so I supposed it made sense. Thinking about the strange balance between dream and reality grounded me a little.

Every step splashed shallow water onto our ankles. The droplets were faintly warm. The cave itself wasn't cold, either—the air felt stagnant, motionless. No matter how far we walked, nothing changed. No exits, no twists, no surprises. Even when I reached out to touch the rock beside me, the texture felt unclear, smudged, as if the dream couldn't be bothered to render it properly.

'This could be a problem,' I muttered to Adachi.

She looked back and simply replied, 'Mm.'

It wasn't as though I was making a serious complaint, but she didn't seem troubled at all. Maybe because we weren't hungry or thirsty. So we just kept walking, side by side.

Water dripped somewhere in the distance. I had never explored a cave in my life, so where had this dream even come from? My feet were submerged directly into water—I couldn't feel my shoes at all. The ground was almost pitch black, and for a moment I feared we'd sink, swallowed endlessly like falling into a bottomless bog.

I tightened my grip on Adachi's hand.

I couldn't remember anything from before we arrived here, but I started wondering what we would do if this wasn't a dream. Above us was nothing but darkness, stretching endlessly, wrapping us in a quiet that felt almost too still.

'Hey… this is a dream, right?' I asked.

'It might be a dream,' Adachi answered.

'I don't remember ever visiting a cave on any trip…'

'It might've been a trip,' she said again, her voice smooth and unbothered, like water flowing wherever it pleased.

'Either way, it doesn't matter to me.'

'Hm?'

'As long as I can reach the end with you, it's fine. Whether we're in a dream or living in the real world, what matters is that we're together.'

She turned back to me.

'I'm happy I get to be here with you, right now.'

She smiled gently. Her expression was calm—none of her usual fluster or confusion. She looked collected, almost mature.

That's… one way to see it, I thought, nodding inwardly.

'But…' I looked around the dim cave. 'If we're going to die anyway, I'd rather do it somewhere warmer. Softer.'

Meaning: in my futon.

'Is that so? Then…'

Adachi wrapped her arms around me, pulling me close, holding me tightly.

Our bodies touched, and for a moment, I thought I saw faint points of light drifting around us like fireflies.

'Warm enough?'

'…Yeah.'

I slipped my arms around her back as well. Since Adachi was taller, I ended up being pressed slightly downward against her.

'Should we just stay like this?'

'…Mm. No—let's walk a little more,' I said. Stopping here would be easy… but that wouldn't be right. I would grow older someday, this body would wither, my mind would change again and again.

And I preferred it that way.

I pulled away from her embrace and took her hand instead, leading her forward this time. Once I got used to the atmosphere, the cave wasn't so unpleasant.

'I really like this part of you, Shimamura,' Adachi said casually beside me.

Ah. So this was a dream.

I finally felt certain—and began to laugh.

'Mm—yeah.'

When I woke up, I slumped against my futon and let the dream sit with me for a while. Adachi had been so… cool. Dependable. Almost dazzling.

Was I hoping for an Adachi like that?

…Didn't feel like a bad thought.

Later, when we were talking on the phone, I decided to tell her: 'Adachi, you should try to be a bit cooler, okay?'

'Eh? Ah—uh—y-yeah. I'll try.'

A perfect answer. That awkward little stutter of hers… it put me at ease all over again.
